coredata.dc:title: Enhanced interfacial electron transfer by constructing NiCo-LDH hollow nanocages decorated N-doped graphene quantum dots heterojunction for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Chenying
Zhang, Long
Liu, Qu
Ding, Yinxuan
Cheng, Lingli
Wu, Minghong
Li, Zhen
coredata.dc:description: Nickel–cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCo-LDHs) are highly promising materials for energy storage electrodes. However, the poor conductivity and easy agglomeration limit their practical application. Herein, the novel hollow nanocage architecture of NiCo-LDH nanosheets (H-NiCo-LDH) decorated N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is constructed using zeolitic imidazolate framework-67 (ZIF-67) as self-sacrificed template. The hollow nanocage structure suppresses the agglomeration of nanosheets and enlarges the electrochemical interface, while the decoration of N-GQDs effectively improves electrical conductivity and provides more abundant active sites. More importantly, HRTEM characterization shows the construction of heterojunctions between H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQDs, and the interfacial charge redistribution through p-n heterojunction promotes interfacial electron transfer and enhances redox activity. Consequently, the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH electrode delivers a high specific capacitance of 2347 F g−1 (326 mA h g−1) at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate performance (82% capacitance retention at 10 A g−1). The assembled asymmetric supercapacitor by N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH and active carbon exhibits an energy density of 52.1 W h kg−1 at a power density of 770 W kg−1 with remarkable cycling stability (80.5% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles). The hollow nanocage structure and enhanced electrical conductivity of N-GQDs make the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH a highly promising material for electrochemical energy storage applications.

head.ce:title: Enhanced interfacial electron transfer by constructing NiCo-LDH hollow nanocages decorated N-doped graphene quantum dots heterojunction for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nickel–cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCo-LDHs) are highly promising materials for energy storage electrodes. However, the poor conductivity and easy agglomeration limit their practical application. Herein, the novel hollow nanocage architecture of NiCo-LDH nanosheets (H-NiCo-LDH) decorated N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is constructed using zeolitic imidazolate framework-67 (ZIF-67) as self-sacrificed template. The hollow nanocage structure suppresses the agglomeration of nanosheets and enlarges the electrochemical interface, while the decoration of N-GQDs effectively improves electrical conductivity and provides more abundant active sites. More importantly, HRTEM characterization shows the construction of heterojunctions between H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQDs, and the interfacial charge redistribution through p-n heterojunction promotes interfacial electron transfer and enhances redox activity. Consequently, the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH electrode delivers a high specific capacitance of 2347 F g−1 (326 mA h g−1) at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate performance (82% capacitance retention at 10 A g−1). The assembled asymmetric supercapacitor by N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH and active carbon exhibits an energy density of 52.1 W h kg−1 at a power density of 770 W kg−1 with remarkable cycling stability (80.5% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles). The hollow nanocage structure and enhanced electrical conductivity of N-GQDs make the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH a highly promising material for electrochemical energy storage applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the requirements of energy storage equipment have increased significantly with the wide application of electronic products [1]. Among new generation of energy storage devices, supercapacitor (SC) is considered as a promising device owing to its large power density, long cyclic life, high safety and low cost [2–4]. However, compared to other electrochemical energy storage equipment, the low energy density of SC limits its further applications [5]. Therefore, much effort has been engaged in the advanced electrode materials to promote the energy density of SC while maintaining its ultrahigh power density and long cyclic life [6].

Nickel–cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCo-LDHs) have attracted considerable attention due to their large theoretical capacity, structural stability during charging and discharging and simple preparation [7,8]. NiCo-LDH nanosheets with the characteristics of interlayer free electron transfer and large number of exposed active sites have been prepared by traditional methods such as solvothermal synthesis, chemical deposition, and electrodeposition [9–11]. However, it is a challenge to control the morphology and structure of NiCo-LDH nanosheets through these methods, resulting in easy agglomeration of the nanosheets, which severely reduces the effective active sites [12].

In order to address the problem, it is a favorable strategy to employ metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) as precursors or self-sacrificed templates for the preparation of NiCo-LDH nanosheets with porous or hollow structures, which provides more exposed active sites and facilitates the transport of electrolyte ions [13–17]. NiCo-LDH nanosheets covered on hollow nanocages derived from zeolitic imidazolate framework-67 (ZIF-67), denoted as H-NiCo-LDH, have been synthesized. Compared to self-assembled NiCo-LDH nanosheets, H-NiCo-LDH composites have higher capacitive performance owing to high surface permeability, porosity, and low mass density [12,18–20]. Wang et al. synthesized H-NiCo-LDH by ZIF-67 as self-sacrificing template and showed a specific capacitance of 1530 F g−1 (49.5% capacitance retention at 10 A g−1) [18]. Lv et al. prepared hollow boxes covered NiCo-LDH nanosheets which delivered a capacitance of 810 C g−1 (1580 F g−1) (51% capacitance retention at 10 A g−1) [19]. Zhang et al. synthesized H-NiCo-LDH hollow nanocage nanostructure with the specific capacitance of 1141 F g−1 (70.1% capacitance retention at 20 A g−1) [12]. From these reported results, H-NiCo-LDH materials exhibited a prominent charge storage capacity due to their special hollow and porous structures. However, it can be pointed out that the specific capacitance of H-NiCo-LDH is still far from the theoretical value of NiCo-LDH (3000 F g−1), especially the low rate performance, which is mainly due to the poor conductivity of NiCo-LDH [21]. To address this problem, we consider the strategy of combining H-NiCo-LDH with carbon materials to increase the pathways for electron transfer and enhance the electrical conductivity. As a new type of functionally zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been highly concerned for the energy storage owing to unique features of high electrode conductivity, small size and rich electrochemically active sites [22–24]. Herein, N-GQDs are anchored onto the surface of H-NiCo-LDH nanosheets to form hierarchical structure composite, which is denoted as N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH. N-GQDs are easily assembled onto H-NiCo-LDH nanosheets due to the small size advantage, and acts as a bridge between adjacent nanosheets, opening up new pathways for electron transfer. More importantly, low Fermi level GQDs can form p-n heterojunction with high Fermi level metal oxides/hydroxides, and the charge redistribution across the p-n junction interface enhances electrons donating/accepting abilities and conductivity [25]. Therefore, it is reasonable to hypothesize that N-GQDs can effectively improve electrochemical performance by increasing electronic conductivity, offering abundant nitrogen redox active sites and enhancing interfacial electron transfer.

Herein, we report a highly devisable strategy to construct hollow nanocages covered uniformly distributed NiCo-LDH nanosheets using ZIF-67 as self-sacrificed template, and N-GQDs further anchor on the surface of NiCo-LDH nanosheets via electrodeposition method to form N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH composite. The hollow nanocage structure of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH effectively suppresses the agglomeration of nanosheets, alleviates the volume expansion during the charge–discharge cycle, meanwhile enlarges the contact surface of electrolyte and electrode and offers more electrochemical active sites. The modification of N-GQDs improves the electronic conductivity of materials, and provides abundant nitrogen redox active sites. Moreover, the interfacial charge redistribution through the p-n heterojunction constructed by p-type N-GQDs and n-type NiCo-LDH enhances redox activity of the composite. The battery-type electrode based on N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH displays superior specific capacitance of 2347 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with ultrahigh rate performance (82% capacitance retention at 10 A g-1). An asymmetric supercapacitor is configured with N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH as the positive electrode and active carbon as the negative electrode, which delivers high energy and power density (52.1 W h kg−1 and 14 kW kg−1) with remarkable cyclic stability.

Cobalt dinitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM), potassium hydroxide (KOH), nitric acid (HNO3), methanol and hydrazine hydrate were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Pyrene was bought from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. All chemicals were analytical reagent and can be used immediately without additional purification.

The N-GQDs were synthesized via a molecular fusion method [26]. First, 4 g pyrene was slowly added into 320 mL HNO3 solution and heated by stirring for 2 day at 80 °C in oil bath. After the reaction, the obtained precursor was washed several times using DI water and then dried at 80 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, 0.4 g obtained precursor was dissolved in 40 mL DI water, and 4 mL hydrazine hydrate solution was added after ultrasonic agitation. The above solution was added into an autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. The N-GQD solution was obtained by multiple filtration to remove impurities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZIF-67, NiCo-LDH and H-NiCo-LDH
ce:section.ce:para: The ZIF-67 nanocrystals were prepared following a reported method [27]. 2.91 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 40 mL methanol to form solution A, while 3.284 g 2-MIM using a similar method to form solution B. Next, the solution B was quickly poured into the solution A, and the mixture solution was stilled to form purple precipitate for 24 h. ZIF-67 powder was collected through a process of centrifugation, washing and drying.

0.6 g Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.06 g of obtained ZIF-67 powder were dissolved in 60 mL EtOH until a homogeneous solution was formed. After that, the solution was added into an 80 mL autoclave and heated at 85℃ for 1 h. In the end, the H-NiCo-LDH powder was obtained after washing with ethanol and DI water. As a comparison sample, NiCo-LDH was fabricated with reference to the literature of Dong et al. [8].

The N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH was fabricated via electrodeposition method. The as-prepared H-NiCo-LDH, acetylene black and PVDF were mixed in 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone solution at a mass ratio of 8:1:1, which was coated on nickel foam (1.0 cm◊1.0 cm) and employed as the working electrode. The electrodeposition was carried out at a constant current of 1 mA for 0.5 h using Pt foil electrode as the counter electrode, and N-GQD solution as the electrolyte. After above steps, the obtained N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH was washed with EtOH and DI water and dried at 70 °C. The average mass loading of the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH on the nickel foam was 1.3 mg cm−2.

The structure and morphology of the materials were measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, JSM-7500F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2010F). The powder X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were completed through Rigaku/MAX-2200VPC (Shimadzu, Japan) using Cu-Kα as radiation source in the 2θ range between 10° and 80°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was conducted at room temperature. The content of N-GQDs in the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH was calculated by thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, TA Instruments, TGA5500) under air conditions.

Electrochemical performance tests including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed across three electrode test system by electrochemical work station (Autolab PGSTAT128). The prepared materials were served as working electrodes, and Pt line and Hg/HgO electrodes were used as reference electrode and counter electrode to investigate electrochemical performance. The mass loading of NiCo-LDH, H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH electrode were 1.295, 1.211 and 1.379 mg cm−2.

The mass of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH and AC applied in the device are 1 and 5 mg according to the above equation. The specific capacitance of the device was counted with formula (1).

The preparation route of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . First, ZIF-67 was synthesized using Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 2-MIM as Co2+ source and organic ligands. Then ZIF-67 as the self-sacrificial template reacted with Ni(NO3)2·6H2O to fabricate hollow NiCo-LDH nanocages covered with nanosheets via hydrothermal and ion exchange process. In this process, H+ was released through the weak hydrolysis of Ni2+, while the 2-MIM ligands of ZIF-67 captured H+ and the dodecahedra was etched, which leaded to the release of Co2+ [28]. The released Co2+ and the external Ni2+ co-deposited on the surface to form NiCo-LDH nanosheets, forming hollow hierarchical NiCo-LDH nanocages [29]. Finally, N-GQDs were deposited on the surface of H-NiCo-LDH nanosheets by electrodeposition process to fabricate N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH composite.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were characterized through SEM and TEM measurements. SEM images (Fig. 2 a-b) shows that the as-prepared ZIF-67 displays smooth dodecahedras with the diameter around 2.5 μm. After reacted with Ni(NO3)2·6H2O during hydrothermal synthesis, the obtained H-NiCo-LDH morphology demonstrates hollow nanocages at the average size of 2.5 μm, as shown in Fig. 2c-d. It can be observed that most H-NiCo-LDH nanocages keep the dodecahedra structure of ZIF-67, while the surfaces of nanocages are covered by uniformly distributed and tightly growing nanosheets. From part of the shattered nanocages (Fig. 2d), it can be noticed that the interior is hollow, proving the hollow structure of nanocages. Compared to the morphology of H-NiCo-LDH, the obtained N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH still remains the hollow nanocages structure with no obvious change (Fig. 2e-f). Fig. 2h-k show the elemental mapping analysis corresponding to the SEM image (Fig. 2g). C, N, Co and Ni elements are dispersed on the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH composite uniformly. For comparison, the SEM image (Fig. S1a) shows that the morphology of NiCo-LDH is flower-like and composed of numerous nanosheets with a size of 10 µm, which is prepared via hydrothermal method without ZIF-67 as self-sacrificial template.

The micro-structure of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH was characterized by TEM and HRTEM in Fig. 3 . It can be noticed that the nanocages are uniform in size and shape (Fig. 3a), while the dark edges and bright inner further confirm the hollow structure of materials. The outer layer of nanosheets (Fig. 3b) are beneficial to exposing more electrochemical active sites, which leads to higher specific capacity. The exposed nanosheets in Fig. 3c shows that numerous N-GQDs are tightly anchored on the surface of nanosheets (marked some N-GQDs in Fig. 3c), while the particle size of N-GQDs is about 4 nm. The lattice fringe spacings showed in the HRTEM image (Fig. 3d) of 0.197 nm, 0.207 nm and 0.23 nm correspond to the (0 1 8), (1 0 7) and (0 1 5) crystal planes of NiCo-LDH [30–32], while 0.21 nm corresponds to (1 0 0) crystal plane of N-GQDs [33]. Significantly, HRTEM image clearly displays the lattice fringes and intimate contact between N-GQDs (1 0 0) and NiCo-LDH (0 1 8) to form high quality interface, demonstrating the successful construction of p-n heterojunctions. In addition, TEM image of NiCo-LDH prepared without ZIF-67 (Fig. S1b) shows a flower-like structure.

XRD measurements were carried out to investigate the composition and phase structures of N-GQDs, H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH (Fig. 4 a). The diffraction peaks located at 26° and 43° are corresponding to (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) plane of N-GQDs [34]. The peaks located at 14.7° (0 0 3), 25.5° (0 0 6), 36.9° (0 1 2), 42.1° (0 1 5) and 63.8° (1 1 0) clearly agree with hexagonal system NiCo-LDH (JCPDS No. 33-0429) [35]. It can be noticed that the diffraction peaks of H-NiCo-LDH widens obviously and the peak position shifts, which may be due to the formation of uniform shell and synchronous etching of sacrificial template resulting in higher residual stress, and the existence of high residual stress widens the diffraction peak and makes the diffraction peak shift [36–38].

As shown in Fig. 4b, the content of N-GQDs in the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH was calculated through TGA. When the temperature increases to 800 °C, three parts of weight losses are exhibited, which occurs in the temperature range of about 30–200 °C, 200–400 °C and 400–800 °C. The first part of weight loss is owing to the strip of physically adsorbed water. The second part is caused by the conversion from H-NiCo-LDH to respective compounds in air atmosphere [39]. Compared to H-NiCo-LDH, the weight loss of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH above 400℃ increases the decomposition fraction of N-GQDs [40]. Judging from this result, the content of N-GQDs in the N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH is estimated to be 4%.

The elemental compositions and chemical states of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH were researched through XPS. From the XPS profiles of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH (Fig. 5 a), the proportion of C, N, O, Ni and Co elements are 39.2, 5.4, 40.5, 10.7 and 4.2 at%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5b, the C 1 s spectrum is fitted into three peaks, attributing to C = C/C–C (284.5 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), and C = O (288.7 eV) [41,42]. The high resolution spectrum of N 1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) is fitted to three peaks at 399.6 eV, 400.9 eV and 402.5 eV, related to pyrrolic N, graphitic N and oxidized N independently, and N-doping is able to augment free electronics and offer rich active sites [43–45]. In Fig. 5d, the O 1s spectrum is fitted into three peaks, attributed to C = O (530.6 eV), C-OH (531.4 eV), and C-O-C (533.0 eV) [46,47]. Fig. 5e exhibits the Ni 2p spectrum, and peaks at binding energy of 855.0 eV and 872.6 eV are in accordance with Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 spin–orbit doublets of Ni2+, together with two major satellite signals at 860.9 eV and 878.8 eV [32,48]. Similar to Ni element, the Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 5f) features with a pair of peaks at 780.9 eV and 796.4 eV correspond to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 spin–orbit doublets of Co2+, together with two major satellite signals at 784.5 eV and 802.5 eV [30,49].

The electrochemical performances of NiCo-LDH, H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH were performed via CV, GCD and EIS measurements in 2 M KOH electrolyte through three electrode system. To acquire the optimal performance of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH, the deposition time of N-GQDs was investigated. The best electrodeposition time for N-GQDs is 30 min at 1 mA (Fig. S2). Fig. 6 a shows the CV curves of NiCo-LDH, H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1 with the potential window between 0 and 0.7 V. Three samples all show battery-type behaviors according to the shape of CV curves, and the related redox reactions can be represented by the following equations [50]:
(6) Ni(OH)2 + OH− ↔ NiOOH + H2O + e−
(7) Co(OH)2 + OH− ↔ CoOOH + H2O + e−

Among all samples, the CV curve of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH displays the biggest scanning area, which demonstrates the robust faradaic behavior as battery-type electrode materials. An apparent potential plateau without obvious voltage drop can be observed in GCD curves (Fig. 6b), in accordance with the redox peaks shown in CV curves, which implies that these materials are typical battery-type electrode materials. The specific capacitance of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH electrode is calculated to be 2347 F g−1 (326 mA h g−1) at 1 A g−1, which is much higher than that of NiCo-LDH (1113 F g−1, 139 mA h g−1) and H-NiCo-LDH (2234 F g−1, 279 mA h g−1), which is also superior than that of previously reported NiCo-LDH based electrode materials, such as NiCo-LDH/GNR (1765 F g−1) [21], His-GQD/NiCo-LDH (1526 F g−1) [51], NiCo-LDH/graphene (1265 F g−1) [52].

As shown in Fig. 6c, all the CV curves shapes for N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH are well maintained at the scan rate from 10 to 50 mV s−1, implying the fast and effective ions transport through the electrode. Fig. 6d shows GCD curves of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH at different current densities. Fig. 6e exhibits the specific capacities of NiCo-LDH, H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH electrodes ranging from 1 to 20 A g−1 according to GCD curves in Fig. S3. About 82.3% of the capacitance is remained for N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH at 10 A g−1, much higher than that of NiCo-LDH (67.5%) and H-NiCo-LDH (68.1%), implying its superior fast discharging capacity, higher rate capability and preferable electrical conductivity with the incorporation of N-GQDs. Fig. 6f shows the Nyquist plots of NiCo-LDH, H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH electrodes in the frequency range from 100 to 0.01 KHz. The intercept along the X-axis at high frequency means the equivalent series resistance (R s), the semi-circle at high-to-medium frequency stands for the charge-transfer resistance (R ct), while the slope at low frequency represents the Warburg resistance (R w) [53–55]. After fitting by the equivalent circuit (inset of Fig. 6f), the value of R s, R ct and R w are displayed in Table S1. The R s, R ct and R w of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH are 0.62 Ω, 2.4 Ω and 0.65 Ω, which are much lower than that of the NiCo-LDH and H-NiCo-LDH, indicating that the hollow structure of NiCo-LDH is beneficial to the electrolyte ions diffusion and transport, meanwhile high conductivity N-GQDs and p-n heterojunctions establish more electron transport pathways to promote the fast electron transfer [56,57]. Fig. S4 illustrates the cycling performance of NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH. The specific capacitance of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH maintains 81.1% of the initial capacitance after 2000 cycles at 10 A g−1, much higher than that of NiCo-LDH (63.7%), indicating that the hollow structure and addition of N-GQDs improve the cycling stability of the electrode.

Table 1 demonstrates the comparison of electrochemical characteristics of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH with reported NiCo-LDH based materials. The N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH shows excellent capacity and ultra-high rate performance compared with other NiCo-LDH based materials because of the increased surface electrochemically active sites of hollow nanocage structure and the improved electrical conductivity of N-GQDs. More importantly, the interfacial electronic structure of p-n heterojunction in N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH with n-type H-NiCo-LDH and p-type N-GQDs can promote interfacial electron transfer and enhance redox activity, improving electrochemical performance of the electrode.

To further understand the structure–function correlation between the heterostructure of H-NiCo-LDH/N-GQDs with the enhanced electrochemical performance, schematic illustration on energy level diagrams, charge transfer between H-NiCo-LDH and p-type N-GQDs, and the construction of p-n heterojunction are shown in Fig. 7 . According to previous literature reports, the band gap of GQDs calculated according to the following formula: E g (eV) = 1.68 eV nm/L, where E g is the energy gap and L is the size of GQDs [66]. Based on our TEM results, the size of N-GQDs is nearly 4 nm, and the energy bandgap of N-GQDs is calculated to be 0.4 eV. The work functions of N-GQDs related to the size of 4 nm is estimated to be 5.26 eV [67]. Furthermore, the energy gap and the work function of H-NiCo-LDH are 1.58 eV and 4.2 eV, respectively [68]. Therefore, the energy level diagrams of H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQDs before contact are illustrated in Fig. 7a. The E f of n-type H-NiCo-LDH (−4.2 eV) is higher than that of p-type N-GQDs (−5.26 eV), which makes it easy for the electrons to transfer from H-NiCo-LDH to N-GQDs across the interface, until the system reaches equilibrium and produces a new Fermi level [69]. In this process, the bending of energy bands at the interface leads to the formation of an electron depletion layer (Fig. 7b), constructing the p-n heterojunction structure [70–72]. When electron transfer is completed and stable p-n heterojunction is formed, electron accumulation on N-GQDs is responsible for improving the reduction activity, while hole accumulation on H-NiCo-LDH is beneficial to improve oxidation activity. Therefore, by means of the internal electric field generated through the p-n junction, the interfacial charge redistribution promotes interfacial electron transfer, enhances redox activity, and improves the charge storage capacity of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH.

The charge storage kinetics behavior of the H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH was employed by recording CV curves (Fig. 6c and Fig. S5a). The relation of current (i) and scan rate (v) can be fitted as following equation [73,74]:
(8)
i = a
v
b
where a and b are constants. When b close to 0.5, the charge storage is diffusion-controlled; When b close to 1, the charge storage is capacitive-controlled. Fig. 8 a demonstrates the relationship of log peak current versus log sweep rate at the potential of 0.34 V. The calculated b-values of H-NiCo-LDH and N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH are 0.53 and 0.56, illustrating that the charge storage mechanisms of both materials are diffusion-controlled. The b value increases with the addition of N-GQDs, illustrating more capacitive energy storage behavior of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH. Besides, the relation of current and sweep rate can quantify the capacitive and diffusion contributions as following formula [75]:
(9)
i
V
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2

k 1 v and k 2 v 1/2 correspond to the surface capacitive-controlled process and diffusion-controlled process. By estimating the values of
k 1
and
k 2
, the contribution of diffusion-controlled and capacitive-controlled can be distinguished [22]. Fig. 8b shows a surface capacitance of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH is 71% at sweep rate of 50 mV s−1. In Fig. 8c, the contribution of surface capacitive increases from 39% to 71% with the increase of the sweep rate. For comparison, the surface contribution of H-NiCo-LDH at different sweep rate are displayed in Fig. 8d and Fig. S5b. The surface contributions of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH are larger than that of H-NiCo-LDH at the equal sweep rate, implying that the decoration of N-GQDs enables the electrode process fast kinetics of charge storage, which is beneficial to improving the rate capacity [24].

To achieve the practical application of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH composite, an asymmetric supercapacitor was configured by N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH as the positive electrode and active carbon as the negative electrode using 2 M KOH as electrolyte. It can be observed in Fig. 9 a that the voltage windows were different for N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH (0 to 0.5 V) and AC (−1 to 0 V), and the potential window of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH//AC asymmetric supercapacitor can reach to be 1.6 V (Fig. 9b). The CV curves of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH//AC at different scan rates are demonstrated in Fig. 9c and its shape are well maintained even at high scanning rates of 100 mV s−1, indicating the fast charge–discharge ability. Meanwhile, GCD profiles of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH//AC (Fig. 9d) delivers a specific capacity of 146 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. About 75% of the capacitance (110 F g−1) is remained at 10 A g−1, implying the excellent rate performance of device (Fig. 9e). As shown in Fig. 9f, Nyquist plots are employed to evaluate the R s (0.78 Ω) and R ct (0.23 Ω) of the device, revealing the low resistance and the effective ion diffusion. Fig. 9g shows the Ragone plot of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH//AC, which exhibits an energy density of 52.1 W h kg−1 at a power density of 770 W kg−1, and the energy density of 30.1 W h kg−1 at an ultrahigh power density of 14000 W kg−1. The energy density of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH//AC asymmetric supercapacitor is higher than most of previously reported NiCo-LDH based device shown in Table S2, such as Ni-Co LDH/rGO/CNTs//AC (34.7 W h kg−1/1550 W kg−1) [76], Ni-Co LDH/G//AC (40.6 W h kg−1/400 W kg−1) [77], and Ni-Co-LDH//AC (28.7 W h kg−1/660 W kg−1) [78]. Fig. 9h exhibits the cyclic stability of the device that retained 82% capacitance of its original numeral at 10 A g−1 for 5000 cycles, meanwhile the coulombic efficiency of the asymmetric supercapacitor is kept at 100.8%.

Based on the overall analysis and discussion, the remarkable electrochemical performance of N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH might be attributed to the following features: (1) The hollow nanocage structure can enlarge the electrochemical interface and provide more active sites, meanwhile suppresses the agglomeration of nanosheets and accommodates the volume expansion; (2) The incorporation of N-GQDs can enhance electrical conductivity and provide more abundant surface electrochemically active sites due to nitrogen doping; (3) The p-n heterojunction constructed by p-type N-GQDs and n-type NiCo-LDH can enhance redox activity through the charge redistribution due to the interfacial electronic structure.

In this work, we have proposed an efficient design of a hierarchical hollow nanocage structure of NiCo-LDH nanosheets using ZIF-67 as self-sacrificed template, anchoring N-doped graphene quantum dots on the surface of nanosheets via electrodeposition process to construct p-n heterojunction. The N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH composite has enhanced electrical conductivity and more abundant surface electrochemically active sites. The N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH electrode delivers an outstanding capacitance of 2347 F g−1 (326 mA h g−1) at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate performance (82% capacitance retention at 10 A g−1). Moreover, the asymmetric N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH//AC supercapacitor delivers high energy and power density (52.1 W h kg−1 and 14 kW kg−1). The remarkable electrochemical performance forecast that N-GQD/H-NiCo-LDH might be a promising new electrode material applied in high-performance supercapacitors.
